quiz_questions.question_text: In an acute asthma exacerbation, the first-line rescue medication is:

options.text: Inhaled short-acting bronchodilator (albuterol) plus systemic corticosteroids
Surgical intervention
Oral antibiotics
Only supplemental oxygen

quiz_questions.explanation: Acute asthma is treated with rapid-acting inhaled beta-2 agonists (albuterol) to relax bronchial smooth muscle, combined with systemic corticosteroids (oral or IV) to reduce the underlying airway inflammation driving the attack.

quiz_questions.question_text: A peak expiratory flow at 50% of predicted value in an asthma patient indicates:

options.text: Restrictive lung disease
Normal airway function
Mild obstruction requiring no treatment
Moderate-to-severe airflow obstruction

quiz_questions.explanation: Peak expiratory flow (PEF) below 50% of predicted indicates significant airflow obstruction, classified as moderate-to-severe in most asthma action plans. This typically requires aggressive treatment with repeated bronchodilators and systemic corticosteroids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Rusty-colored sputum in a pneumonia patient classically suggests infection by:

options.text: Mycobacterium tuberculosis
Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcal pneumonia)
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Influenza virus

quiz_questions.explanation: Rusty (blood-tinged) sputum is a classic finding in pneumococcal pneumonia caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae. The rusty color comes from red blood cells mixing with the inflammatory exudate in the alveoli.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ABG showing pH 7.28 and PaCO₂ of 60 mmHg in a COPD patient indicates:

options.text: Respiratory acidosis from CO₂ retention (hypercapnia)
Respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation
Metabolic alkalosis
Normal acid-base balance

quiz_questions.explanation: The low pH (<7.35) combined with elevated PaCO₂ (>45 mmHg) defines respiratory acidosis. In this COPD patient, impaired ventilation is causing CO₂ retention, and the accumulated carbonic acid is driving the pH below normal.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a COPD patient who relies on hypoxic drive, the oxygen saturation target is typically:

options.text: 100% on high-flow oxygen
88-92% (using controlled, low-flow oxygen)

quiz_questions.explanation: For COPD patients with chronic CO₂ retention who depend on the hypoxic drive, the target SpO₂ is 88-92%. Over-correcting hypoxemia with excessive oxygen can suppress their breathing stimulus, worsen hypoventilation, and cause dangerous CO₂ narcosis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Asthma is classified as which type of lung disease?

options.text: Mixed obstructive and restrictive
Restrictive
Obstructive (reversible airway narrowing causing difficulty with exhalation)
Neither obstructive nor restrictive

quiz_questions.explanation: Asthma is a classic obstructive lung disease: bronchospasm, mucosal edema, and mucus plugging narrow the airways and increase resistance to airflow, particularly during expiration. The key distinction is that asthma's obstruction is largely reversible with treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spirometry in obstructive lung disease characteristically shows:

options.text: Increased FVC with normal FEV₁
Reduced FEV₁/FVC ratio (below 70%)
All values are normal
Normal FEV₁/FVC ratio

quiz_questions.explanation: In obstructive disease, the patient cannot exhale quickly because of increased airway resistance. FEV₁ is disproportionately reduced compared to FVC, producing an FEV₁/FVC ratio below 70%, which is the diagnostic criterion for airflow obstruction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A COPD exacerbation is most commonly triggered by:

options.text: Adequate sleep
Routine physical exercise
Respiratory infections (viral or bacterial) or environmental irritants
Eating large meals

quiz_questions.explanation: Respiratory tract infections (especially viral upper respiratory infections and bacterial bronchitis) are the most common triggers of COPD exacerbations. Air pollution, temperature changes, and non-adherence to maintenance medications are additional triggers.

quiz_questions.question_text: Right lower lobe consolidation on a chest X-ray in a pneumonia case means:

options.text: Alveoli in the right lower lobe are filled with fluid, pus, or inflammatory exudate
The right lower lobe has normal air-filled alveoli
A tumor is obstructing the right lower lobe bronchus
The right lower lobe has collapsed from a pneumothorax

quiz_questions.explanation: Consolidation means the normally air-filled alveoli have become solidified with inflammatory material. On chest X-ray, this appears as a dense white opacity in the affected lobe, confirming the location and extent of the pneumonia.

quiz_questions.question_text: Long-term management of COPD includes all of the following EXCEPT:

options.text: Pulmonary rehabilitation and vaccinations (flu, pneumococcal)
Curative surgery to reverse all lung damage
Smoking cessation
Inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids

quiz_questions.explanation: COPD management focuses on slowing progression and managing symptoms through smoking cessation, pharmacotherapy, pulmonary rehabilitation, and vaccination. There is no curative surgery that reverses the damage, although lung volume reduction surgery or transplant may be considered in select severe cases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is empiric antibiotic therapy?

options.text: Initiating antibiotic treatment based on the most likely pathogens and clinical presentation before culture and sensitivity results are available
Using the most expensive antibiotic available regardless of the infection
Waiting until culture results are confirmed before starting any treatment
Administering antibiotics for viral infections

quiz_questions.explanation: Empiric therapy means starting treatment based on educated clinical judgment about the most probable causative organisms before definitive laboratory results are available. Once culture and sensitivity results return, the antibiotic can be narrowed or changed for targeted therapy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'FEV₁' mean?

options.text: The fraction of inspired oxygen being delivered
Forced expiratory volume in one second — the volume of air a patient can forcefully exhale in the first second after a maximal inhalation
The total volume of air the lungs can hold
The volume of air remaining after normal expiration

quiz_questions.explanation: FEV₁ (forced expiratory volume in one second) is one of the most important spirometric measurements. It reflects how quickly air can be expelled from the lungs and is used along with FVC to calculate the FEV₁/FVC ratio for diagnosing and staging obstructive lung diseases.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a sputum culture?

options.text: A laboratory test where expectorated mucus is grown on culture media to identify the specific pathogen causing a respiratory infection and determine antibiotic sensitivity
A radiological imaging study of the chest
A blood test to measure oxygen levels
A pulmonary function test measuring airflow rates

quiz_questions.explanation: A sputum culture involves collecting a sample of coughed-up mucus and growing it in the laboratory to identify the specific bacteria, fungi, or other organisms responsible for the infection. Sensitivity testing then determines which antibiotics will effectively kill the identified pathogen.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'exacerbation' mean in the context of chronic respiratory disease?

options.text: A gradual improvement in symptoms over time
An acute worsening (flare-up) of symptoms beyond normal day-to-day variation, often requiring intensified treatment
Complete cure of the disease
The initial diagnosis of a new disease

quiz_questions.explanation: An exacerbation is an acute episode of symptom worsening in a chronic disease. In COPD or asthma, this means a sudden increase in dyspnea, cough, sputum production, or wheezing that goes beyond the patient's normal fluctuations and typically requires a change in medication or hospitalization.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is pulmonary rehabilitation?

options.text: A medication regimen consisting only of inhaled bronchodilators
A surgical procedure to remove damaged lung tissue

A comprehensive program of exercise training, education, behavior change, and psychosocial support designed to improve the physical and psychological condition of people with chronic respiratory diseases
A diagnostic procedure to identify lung pathogens

quiz_questions.explanation: Pulmonary rehabilitation is a multidisciplinary program that combines supervised exercise, breathing techniques, nutritional counseling, and patient education. It has been proven to improve exercise tolerance, reduce dyspnea, enhance quality of life, and decrease hospitalizations in COPD patients.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'asthma action plan'?

options.text: A list of foods to avoid to prevent asthma attacks
A schedule for annual spirometry testing only
A written, individualized management guide using a color-coded zone system (green/yellow/red) that instructs patients on how to adjust medications based on symptoms and peak flow readings
A surgical plan for removing the patient's lung

quiz_questions.explanation: An asthma action plan is a personalized, written document developed with the patient's physician that uses a traffic-light system: green zone (well-controlled, take maintenance meds), yellow zone (worsening, increase treatment), and red zone (emergency, seek immediate medical care).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'community-acquired pneumonia' (CAP) mean?

options.text: Pneumonia contracted while hospitalized or in a healthcare facility
A mild upper respiratory infection that does not affect the lungs
Pneumonia caused exclusively by Pseudomonas bacteria
Pneumonia acquired outside of a hospital or healthcare setting, in the general community

quiz_questions.explanation: Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) develops in patients who have not been recently hospitalized or exposed to healthcare environments. The most common pathogens differ from hospital-acquired pneumonia and typically include S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, atypical organisms, and respiratory viruses.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'wheezing' indicate?

options.text: Normal air movement in healthy lungs
Fluid in the pleural space
A high-pitched musical sound produced by air flowing through narrowed airways, typically heard during expiration and indicating airway obstruction
Air moving through consolidated lung tissue

quiz_questions.explanation: Wheezing is a continuous, high-pitched musical breath sound caused by turbulent airflow through narrowed bronchi or bronchioles. It is characteristically heard during expiration (though severe obstruction may produce inspiratory wheezing) and is a hallmark of asthma and COPD exacerbations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'bronchoscopy'?

options.text: A blood test measuring inflammatory markers from the bronchi
An X-ray of the chest focused on the bronchi
A procedure in which a thin, flexible scope is inserted through the nose or mouth into the airways to directly visualize the bronchial tree, obtain tissue samples, or remove obstructions
A spirometry test specifically measuring bronchial airflow

quiz_questions.explanation: Bronchoscopy is a diagnostic and therapeutic procedure that allows direct visualization of the tracheobronchial tree using a flexible or rigid scope. It can be used to biopsy suspicious lesions, collect bronchoalveolar lavage samples, remove foreign bodies, or place stents in obstructed airways.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'clinical reasoning' mean in pulmonology case studies?

options.text: The systematic process of integrating patient history, physical examination, laboratory data, imaging, and spirometry to arrive at an accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment plan
Using only chest X-rays to diagnose all respiratory conditions
Prescribing antibiotics for every respiratory complaint
Making treatment decisions based solely on one test result

quiz_questions.explanation: Clinical reasoning in pulmonology requires synthesizing information from multiple sources — patient symptoms, physical exam findings (breath sounds, respiratory pattern), spirometry, ABGs, imaging (CXR, CT), and microbiology — to build a differential diagnosis and determine the best evidence-based treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 28-year-old woman with known asthma presents to the ER during pollen season with severe dyspnea, audible wheezing, and inability to speak in full sentences. Her peak flow is 30% of predicted. After three rounds of nebulized albuterol, her peak flow improves only to 40%. The physician considers this a severe, poorly responsive attack. What should be the next step in management?

options.text: Administer IV corticosteroids, ipratropium bromide, consider IV magnesium sulfate, and prepare for possible intubation if she continues to deteriorate
Switch to oral antibiotics for a suspected infection
Discharge her home with an inhaler and follow-up in one week
Perform immediate bronchoscopy to visualize the obstruction

quiz_questions.explanation: A severe asthma attack poorly responsive to initial bronchodilator therapy requires escalation: systemic corticosteroids, addition of ipratropium (anticholinergic bronchodilator), IV magnesium sulfate (smooth muscle relaxant), continuous monitoring, and preparation for intubation and mechanical ventilation if respiratory failure develops.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 65-year-old man with a 45-pack-year smoking history presents with increased sputum production, worsening dyspnea, and a low-grade fever for 3 days. His baseline SpO₂ is 87% on room air. ABG shows pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 58, PaO₂ 52, HCO₃⁻ 30. The elevated bicarbonate suggests this patient's respiratory acidosis is:

options.text: Metabolic in origin, not respiratory
Acute and uncompensated
Fully compensated with a normal pH
Chronic with partial renal compensation — the kidneys have retained bicarbonate over time to buffer the chronic CO₂ retention

quiz_questions.explanation: The elevated HCO₃⁻ (30 mEq/L, above normal 22-26) indicates that the kidneys have been compensating for chronic respiratory acidosis by retaining bicarbonate to buffer the excess acid. However, since the pH is still below 7.35, compensation is partial, likely with an acute-on-chronic exacerbation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50-year-old woman presents with fever, productive cough with greenish sputum, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. Her chest X-ray shows right middle lobe consolidation. Blood cultures are drawn and empiric antibiotics are started. Sputum culture later grows Streptococcus pneumoniae sensitive to amoxicillin. What is the significance of the sensitivity result?

options.text: It means the organism is resistant to amoxicillin and a different antibiotic must be used
It confirms that the organism will be effectively killed by amoxicillin, allowing the physician to narrow therapy from a broad-spectrum to a targeted antibiotic
It indicates the pneumonia is viral and antibiotics are unnecessary
It means the patient is allergic to amoxicillin

quiz_questions.explanation: Antibiotic sensitivity testing shows which drugs effectively inhibit or kill the identified pathogen. A 'sensitive' result means the organism is susceptible to that antibiotic at achievable concentrations. This allows de-escalation from broad-spectrum empiric therapy to targeted treatment, reducing side effects and antibiotic resistance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 35-year-old construction worker falls from a scaffold and suffers multiple rib fractures on the left side. He is splinting that side and taking very shallow breaths to avoid pain. His SpO₂ drops to 89%. The trauma surgeon explains that his shallow breathing pattern is dangerous. Why are rib fractures particularly harmful to pulmonary function?

options.text: Rib fractures cause permanent COPD
The broken ribs puncture the alveoli directly
The fractures block blood flow to the left lung
Pain-induced splinting reduces tidal volume and prevents deep breaths, leading to hypoventilation, atelectasis, and potential pneumonia from retained secretions

quiz_questions.explanation: Rib fractures cause severe pain with each breath, causing the patient to voluntarily limit chest wall movement (splinting). This results in shallow breathing, reduced tidal volume, poor cough, and mucus retention. If not managed with adequate pain control and incentive spirometry, the patient can develop atelectasis and secondary pneumonia.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 22-year-old college athlete collapses during practice. In the ER, he is found to have a spontaneous left pneumothorax. A chest tube is inserted with immediate re-expansion of the lung. The athlete asks the physician why thin, tall young men are at higher risk for this condition. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: Tall, thin individuals often have blebs (small air-filled blisters) at the lung apices where distending pressures are highest; rupture of these blebs allows air into the pleural space
Exercise always causes pneumothorax in young men
Their ribs are more likely to fracture and puncture the lung
Tall people have weaker immune systems that make their lungs more fragile

quiz_questions.explanation: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax typically occurs in tall, thin young males. The prevailing theory is that their lung apices experience higher distending pressures (due to the gravitational gradient in a taller chest), promoting the formation of subpleural blebs that can rupture spontaneously.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 70-year-old nursing home resident develops fever, confusion, and a productive cough. Chest X-ray shows bilateral lower lobe infiltrates. The geriatrician suspects aspiration pneumonia. The resident has a history of dysphagia from a previous stroke. Why are stroke patients particularly prone to aspiration pneumonia?

options.text: Stroke patients cannot cough at all
Stroke destroys the alveoli making them vulnerable to infection
Stroke directly suppresses the immune system in the lungs
Stroke can impair swallowing reflexes and gag reflex (dysphagia), allowing oral secretions and food particles to enter the airways and introduce bacteria into the lungs

quiz_questions.explanation: Stroke can damage the brainstem or cortical areas controlling swallowing, resulting in dysphagia and an impaired gag reflex. When oral secretions, food, or gastric contents are aspirated past the impaired protective mechanisms into the lungs, they introduce bacteria and cause aspiration pneumonia.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ICU patient on mechanical ventilation for ARDS develops a new fever and purulent secretions on day 5 of intubation. Sputum cultures grow a multidrug-resistant organism. The infectious disease physician diagnoses ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). Why are intubated patients at high risk for pneumonia?

options.text: The endotracheal tube bypasses upper airway defenses, prevents effective coughing, allows bacterial colonization along the tube, and microaspiration of contaminated secretions around the cuff
The ventilator eliminates all surfactant from the lungs
The ventilator delivers bacteria directly into the lungs
Mechanical ventilation destroys the alveolar surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Endotracheal intubation bypasses the normal upper airway defense mechanisms (nasal filtration, mucociliary clearance, cough reflex), provides a direct pathway for bacteria to reach the lower airways, and allows microaspiration of secretions pooling above the tube cuff. These factors make ventilator-associated pneumonia a serious ICU complication.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physician is reviewing the case of a 55-year-old man with newly diagnosed lung cancer. The patient asks about staging and prognosis. The physician explains that a CT scan showed a 4-cm mass in the right upper lobe with mediastinal lymph node involvement but no distant metastases. Why is mediastinal lymph node involvement significant for treatment planning?

options.text: It indicates the cancer is benign and requires no treatment

Mediastinal lymph node involvement indicates regional spread beyond the primary tumor, which affects surgical resectability and often necessitates chemotherapy and/or radiation in addition to or instead of surgery
Lymph node involvement has no impact on treatment decisions
It means the cancer has already metastasized to the brain

quiz_questions.explanation: Mediastinal (N2/N3) lymph node involvement indicates that the cancer has spread beyond the lung parenchyma, which significantly impacts staging, surgical candidacy, and prognosis. In many cases, it shifts treatment from surgery alone to multimodal therapy involving chemotherapy, radiation, and possibly immunotherapy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A child with cystic fibrosis is hospitalized for a severe pulmonary exacerbation. The respiratory therapist performs chest physiotherapy (CPT) and postural drainage four times daily. A nursing student asks how these techniques help the patient. What is the physiological basis of chest physiotherapy in CF?

options.text: CPT strengthens the diaphragm to improve breathing mechanics
CPT increases surfactant production to keep alveoli open

Percussion and vibration of the chest wall, combined with gravity-assisted positioning, help mobilize the thick, tenacious mucus from the smaller airways to the larger airways where it can be coughed out
CPT directly kills the bacteria in the mucus

quiz_questions.explanation: In CF, the thick mucus overwhelms the impaired mucociliary clearance. Chest physiotherapy uses manual or mechanical percussion to loosen the adherent mucus from airway walls, while postural drainage uses gravity to move the loosened secretions toward the larger central airways where they can be expectorated.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is presenting a pneumonia case to the attending physician. The student reports the patient's history, exam findings (fever, productive cough, crackles on auscultation), chest X-ray results (left lower lobe consolidation), lab work (elevated WBC, positive blood cultures), and ABG values. The attending commends the student for a thorough presentation. Why is integrating all these data sources important in pulmonology case management?

options.text: Each data source provides a different piece of the clinical puzzle — history reveals risk factors, exam identifies physical signs, imaging localizes the disease, labs identify the pathogen, and ABG assesses severity — together enabling the most accurate diagnosis and treatment plan
Presenting multiple findings only serves to impress the attending without clinical benefit
The data sources are redundant and any single one would suffice
Only one data source is ever needed for a respiratory diagnosis

quiz_questions.explanation: No single test or finding can provide a complete clinical picture. Integration of history (risk factors, symptom timeline), physical exam (breath sounds, respiratory pattern), imaging (location and extent of disease), laboratory data (pathogen identification, inflammatory markers), and ABG (severity of gas exchange impairment) allows the clinician to formulate the most accurate diagnosis and optimal treatment strategy.
